additional_notes: German racing game with 12 different race tracks selectable via button press before race start. Tracks become progressively more difficult, with selection restarting from easiest track after completing all 12. Single player drives white car while computer controls black and gray cars. Two-player mode has one player with white car and second player with black car, computer controls gray cars only. Scoring system awards 10 points per completed lap when passing certain invisible checkpoints. Game features collision detection with sound effects and temporary stopping when hitting other cars or oil slicks. Driver can correct course by counter-steering. Players steer with steering wheel, accelerate by pressing gas pedal with right foot, and select between slow and fast gears with gear lever. Point totals displayed continuously at screen edge with different point thresholds extending game time automatically. Described as typical competitive racing game providing strong player appeal and excitement for anyone who can drive a car.

controls.buttons: Club Select button for shot improvement
controls.notes: Clubs for each shot are selected automatically by the game for the beginner. For the expert the Club Select button will allow you to improve on your shot.

coin_mechanism: One coin placed in the game will allow you to play three holes. Bonus holes are awarded for getting par or better. The player can only play the front 9 holes (no matter how many bonus holes are won) and will be asked to continue to play the back 9. Or the player can insert 5 coins to guarantee a complete 9 hole round thus making the game actually two games in one. The game always ends after the 18th hole (no matter how many bonus holes are won).
additional_notes: This challenging 18 hole golf course has excellent graphics and provides the realism of an actual golf course. Course has sand traps, water traps and wind that will make your day at the course a real challenge. A timer insures that the player will tee-off promptly. If the player goes twice par, he must give up the hole. Warning about power supply conversion: Under no circumstances directly connect any harness of this type into the new board set without making necessary changes to overcome any board failures. Equipment generates and can radiate radio frequency energy - FCC compliance required.

This is a press release dated September 18, 1981 announcing that Atari's Centipede video game received UL (Underwriters Laboratory) certification. Atari was noted as the first coin-operated games manufacturer to actively pursue and obtain UL approval for one of its video games. The document mentions this was Atari's most recent coin-operated video game introduction at the time and includes a quote from Frank A. Ballouz, vice president of marketing for the Coin-Operated Games Division, stating Atari's commitment to consumer safety and plans to submit all future games for UL approval.

controls.notes: Trackball skipping issue addressed in bulletin

additional_notes: Service bulletin addressing two issues: 1) Trackball skipping - replace resistors at positions R-84, R-86, R-131, R-133 with 6.8K OHM resistors (only needed on A-F board revisions, later boards have correct values). 2) Intermittent no fire or no coin/credit - replace resistors R-115, R-117, R-119, and R-120 from 1K OHM to 470 OHM values.

Modification involves cutting trace between pins 18 & 19 of LS377 at location 4D, and adding jumpers from pin 1 and pin 2 of 74S04 at location 3A to feed-through points and custom integrated circuit at location 1/2C. additional_notes: Service bulletin dated 12/27/77 describes method for locating bad RAM using Figure One TV display. When one or more columns are missing or altered in the display pattern, this indicates a RAM problem. The display shows numbered columns (15-14-13-12-11-10-9-8-15-14-13-12-11-10-9-8) with sections marked G and H for RAM location identification. This diagnostic display helps technicians visually identify which RAM chips are faulty by observing missing or corrupted vertical columns in the test pattern.

Game features attract mode, crash mode, and rating system (License Revoked 0-10, Backseat Driver 11-20, Good 21-30, Excellent 31-40, Real Pro Above 40). Default play time is 1 minute 40 seconds. PCB edge connector uses 1¼ inch hole located at left lower bottom portion of cabinet. WD-40 silicone lubricant recommended for coin acceptors and rejector linkages maintenance.

This is a dual game manual covering both Head On 2 and Invinco. Contains important service note that dropping a coin into the mechanism will reset the system and clear temporary problems caused by power line disturbances or static. The circuitry has been arranged so that quarter insertion through coin mechanism will reset the system. If coin counter problems occur and no replacement is available, the game will function properly if a 10K Ohm resistor is connected across the coin counter input pins to the video logic board. Manual emphasizes that field repair attempts without express factory consent will void warranty. Uses digital integrated circuitry, microprocessor, TTL digital integrated circuitry and T.V. monitor concepts.

This is an electronic game that makes extensive use of digital integrated circuitry and television monitor circuitry. Manual includes operating instructions, service manual, repackaging instructions, game concept, maintenance procedures, transformer voltage conversion instructions, and schematics. Warning states that any attempt to repair the game in the field without express consent of the factory will immediately void the warranty. Manual emphasizes never replacing components with anything other than exact replacement parts, never removing circuit boards/connections while power is on, and never replacing fuses with improper values as a blown fuse indicates an overload condition. pcbs.name: Main PCB
pcbs.description: Main board with power connection P27 and diagnostic LEDs

additional_notes: Normal boot sequence: LED3 flashes red initially, DONE LED turns red then off, LEDs 1,2,3 turn red with LED3 flashing, LEDs 1&2 turn off while LED3 stays on, individual LEDs 1,2,3 cycle red then off, LED2 blinks red with PIC LED turning red and HDD LED blinking, LED2 continues blinking with PIC LED getting brighter and HDD LED blinking during game demonstration. Hard drive connectivity uses different colored connectors - blue for main board, grey for hard drives. LED HD indicates hard drive status - dim/not flashing indicates no power or bad drive, not flashing/on indicates bad ribbon cable or bad drive. Power connection at P27 has indicator LEDs for +5, +12, -5 voltages.

key_ics.description: Device for slow motion fix on DE-0190-0/1 PCBs
Device for slow motion fix on DE-0190-2 PCB

coin_mechanism: Coin door assembly with wiring harness modifications required. Coin 1 and Coin 2 socket switching modifications needed for proper credit registration.
additional_notes: Document details technical problems and solutions including: 1) Coinage problems where coin meter registers but game resets to attraction mode instead of giving credits, 2) Slow motion video issues (1/8 normal speed) requiring IC modifications and capacitor additions, 3) EPROM speed verification required - 200, 250, or 300 nano-second EPROMs work, but 350 or 450 nano-second EPROMs are too slow. Solutions include IC modifications, capacitor additions (10 MicroFarad 16V tantalum/aluminum), wire jumpers, and grounding modifications. Serial numbers higher than 261000 produced by Dynamo require different grounding wire routing.

additional_notes: This is a comprehensive troubleshooting manual for Midway's processor boards from July 1976. It includes a systematic flow chart for diagnosing bad boards, starting with picture quality assessment and proceeding through various test cases including RAM tests, vertical line checks, and program verification. The manual covers multiple failure scenarios: Case A (Good Picture - Bad Program), Case B (Bad Picture with subcases for good/bad/no vertical lines), Case C (No Picture - Dead), and Case D (Bad Cowboy). The document includes detailed test procedures, clock section checks, pin configurations, logic diagrams, and mother board schematics. This appears to be part of Midway's early arcade system documentation, predating many of their famous titles. The troubleshooting approach is methodical and designed for technicians to systematically isolate processor board failures in arcade systems.

Includes diagnostic test pattern (DP-159-01) with solid red screen and white lines/dots. Adjustments require qualified color-TV repair technician and take as long as an hour with two people. Monitor should warm up 30 minutes before adjustments. Game's upper rear access panel provides access to neck PCB and light board.

additional_notes: Technical bulletin addresses flat target photo sensor damage issue. Flat targets should be angled downwards by approximately 10 degrees to prevent target cover from hitting photo sensor. Repair procedure involves removing upper rear door, disconnecting connectors CN20, CN21, CN22, and CN29, removing flange socket head screws (M4x6 and M5x8), and adjusting target angle by bending downwards. Photo sensor should have 5-10mm clearance from target cover when solenoid operates.

additional_notes: Game logic schematic showing digital circuit implementation with TTL logic gates, counters, multiplexers, and memory components. Contains memory interface circuitry, address decoding logic, and control signal generation circuits typical of early arcade game hardware.

Service bulletin covers RAM, PROM and ROM testing procedures. The shuffleboard game microprocessor system has been programmed to detect defective RAM, PROM and/or ROM. Test requires setting switch #8 to on position and activating coin door tilt switch. Ram test sequence scans vertical lines - when defective, dark vertical columns appear indicating bad RAM location. When RAM test is completed and all RAMs are good, TV screen will blank out to indicate bad PROM or ROM by displaying letters locating it. Includes Figure One showing method of locating bad RAM with numbered columns 15-14-13-12-11-10-9-8-15-14-13-12-11-10-9-8 for RAM location identification. Document dated 12/27/77 and signed by Andy Ducay (AD/jk).

monitor.notes: Uses television monitor concepts

additional_notes: Electronic game that makes extensive use of digital circuitry and television monitor concepts. Uses Gremlin's new G-80 system which makes troubleshooting easy with separate function boards that can be isolated individually. Each board has a separate function and most problems can be immediately isolated to a single board. Individual boards contain less circuitry than if they were combined on one or two boards, resulting in faster and easier servicing. The G-80 system gives Gremlin flexibility in designing new games. additional_notes: Solid-state circuitry with PCB connections that must be secure. Warning against servicing with ordinary testing equipment due to internal voltage that may damage solid-state circuitry. Never connect or disconnect solid-state modules while power is on. Erroneous or partial score may appear on screen when first switched on, corrects automatically when first play starts. Game features breakout-style gameplay with blocks worth different points (1st-2nd rows: 1 pt, 3rd-4th rows: 3 pts, 5th-6th rows: 5 pts, 7th-8th rows: 7 pts). Ball speed increases when hitting blocks on 5th-8th rows. Paddle shortens when ball clears opening in wall. Bonus replay awarded at 400 points (adjustable). Game ends after 3 balls served to each player (adjustable to 5 balls per player). Sound effects include bounce, blip, and buzzing sounds.

additional_notes: Clock divider divides φ2 (phase two) clock frequency downward approximately near 706 Khz to approximately 10 Khz for proper musical tone sequence generation. Clock divider consists of counters 1A and 1B with preload combination dividing φ2 clock by 71. search_blob: 136002-217 Main PCB Upright / Cocktail

additional_notes: This is a service bulletin dated November 19, 1981 explaining the Skill-Step feature for Tempest arcade games. The Skill-Step feature allows players to select their starting level based on their previous highest level achieved. Players cannot access higher levels until they successfully complete lower levels. The bulletin includes detailed level progression charts showing which levels become available based on the highest level reached in the previous game. Level selection occurs at the beginning of every game with a 10-second timer for selection and 3-second warning. Players choose levels by spinning the knob until a white box surrounds the desired level, then pressing fire/superzapper or waiting for timer expiration. Level choices are determined by attract mode cycles and previous game performance.

additional_notes: High importance service bulletin addressing fire hazard. Issue caused by improper standoffs being over-tightened during assembly, compressing and potentially fracturing PCB. This can create shorts between 5V and GND circuits. Serial numbers affected: 4357-6007 and older games 1000-4356. Remedy involves relieving over-tightening, replacing standoffs if necessary, and adding fuse-holder. Games should be turned off until safety harness is installed. 15-minute upgrade procedure requires Phillips-head screwdriver or 1/4 inch hex driver and pliers. Contact Betson Enterprises at 1-800-753-2513 for assistance.

additional_notes: VS.GOLF contains 6 Software ROMs and 1 Custom IC. All Software ROMs and Custom ICs must be inserted in both portions on the P.C. Board. The game can be interchanged between left and right side. For VS. DualSystem, dip switch settings will be on either SW1 or SW2 depending on which side of the p.c. board you install the EPROMs and custom IC. For VS. UniSystem, the EPROMs and custom IC must be installed in the sub side of the p.c. board, and then SW2 is used. Game includes stroke play and match play modes with various scoring adjustments for golf scenarios including Hole in One, Eagle, Birdie, Par, Bogey, and Double Bogey & Over.

controls.buttons: 1 firing button
controls.notes: Control the fighter with joystick and zap monsters with firing button

additional_notes: Exciting pursuit game that combines colorful action with reward system for skillful play. Game begins on Vacuum World where player zaps monsters. Fighter can enter warp zone in center of playfield to battle in Maze World. Weapons are time-delay bombs dropped by pressing and releasing firing button. More monsters destroyed with single bomb earns more bonus points. Monsters can catch fighter on either world, and vacuum world monsters also fire missiles. Number of fighters set by operator. Game ends when all fighters destroyed. Manual includes sections for operators covering installation, maintenance, and operation, plus technician information for troubleshooting and parts lists with assembly drawings, schematics, and wiring diagrams.
